data.title: Commodity Roundup: Oil futures face weekly fall, global uncertainty cuts diesel prices
data.description:
Crude oil prices faced their first weekly fall in three on prospects of more OPEC+ supply, while all three transportation fuel markets—gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel—are experiencing downward price pressures in 2025 due to a weaker macroeconomic outlook emerging as a result of higher US tariffs.

Brent crude (CO1:COM) held just above $64/bbl at the time of writing, down nearly 2% this week, while WTI futures (CL1:COM) fell more than 2% so far this week. The price drivers have been mixed so far. 

Crude oil prices settled lower for a third straight session on Thursday, as the possibility of another large production increase by OPEC+ for July added to concerns that global supply could outpace demand growth.

Despite the recent pullback in tariff levels from the US and China in May, the enactment of reciprocal tariffs by the Trump administration has triggered significant economic concerns, Fitch's BMI Research said.

"This has directly impacted Brent crude prices, which fell about 12.5% in early April 2025, causing corresponding declines across fuel markets," the brokerage added.

BMI has a Brent target of 68/bbl in 2025 and $71/bbl in 2026, down from $80/bbl in 2024.  

Similarly, diesel prices in Singapore, Rotterdam, and New York decreased in line with crude oil declines, with BMI forecasting global diesel prices to average around $83/bbl in 2025 and weaken to $82/bbl in 2026.

 

 

Jet fuel prices have followed a similar trajectory, with expectations to decline to an average of $83.6/bbl in 2025, representing a 15.8% decrease from $99.3/bbl in 2024.

"Refining margins across all fuel types are narrowing as the post-pandemic boom in demand gives way to more balanced or oversupplied market conditions, with supply growth outpacing demand."

On a YTD basis, U.S. refiner PBF Energy (PBF) -22%, Delek US Holdings (DK) +6.2%, Phillips 66 (PSX) -0.83%, CVR Energy (CVI) +28%, Valero Energy (VLO) +5% and Marathon Petroleum (MPC) 13.9%.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

data.title: Foxconn among potential bidders in $3B deal for chip assembly company UTAC - report
data.description:
Foxconn Technology (OTCPK:FXCOF) is among potential bidders for UTAC Holdings in a transaction that could value the Singapore-based semiconductor assembly and testing company at about $3B, Reuters reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

Chinese private equity firm Wise Road Capital — which owns UTAC — has hired Jefferies to run a sale process and anticipates to take non-binding bids by the end of May, the report added.

Foxconn and UTAC did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

UTAC could see interest from financial and strategic bidders which are not from the U.S. because of the China presence, the report noted.

The U.S. and China have been in a trade tussle and only recently came to a tentative agreement to reduce the tariffs imposed on each other’s goods. Last week, the two superpowers agreed to a deal that would see each country cut tariffs by 115%. Besides tariffs, the U.S. has imposed curbs on the export of advanced chips used for AI to China.

The Apple (AAPL) supplier — which is formally known as Hon Hai Precision (OTCPK:HNHAF) (OTCPK:HNHPF) — in recent years has expanded into making semiconductors as part of its long-term growth strategy, as per its website.

UTAC provides assembly and test services for diverse semiconductor devices, according to the company's website.

UTAC does not disclose its financial results but its estimated annual earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, or EBITDA, are about $300M, the report noted.

data.title: Mullen Group prices C$400M senior notes
Tuniu gets non-compliance notice from Nasdaq
data.description:
• Tuniu (NASDAQ:TOUR) said it has received a written notification from the Nasdaq stock market, stating that the firm is not in compliance with the minimum bid price rule. 
  • The firm has until November 17 to regain compliance.

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow futures muted, yields flat as focus stays on fiscal situation
data.description:
Stock index futures were muted on Friday, while yields were flat, as focus remained on the country's fiscal situation ahead of the long weekend.

S&P 500 futures (SPX), Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) and Dow futures (INDU) were all flat.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 1 basis point to 4.53%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) was unchanged at 4%.

Wall Street's major averages ended largely flat on Thursday, while a bruising bond sell-off from the previous session eased up. The focus remained on Trump's “One Big Beautiful” tax bill, which was narrowly passed by the U.S. House.

U.S. Treasury yields stabilized on Thursday after soaring on Wednesday, as traders had dumped bonds amid concerns over the new tax bill's effects on the ballooning U.S. fiscal deficit.

“After a heavy selloff on Wednesday, markets began to stabilize again over the last 24 hours, despite investors’ ongoing fears about the fiscal situation,” Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

One factor helping U.S. assets to stabilize was some stronger U.S. economic data, which continued to point away from a recession, Allen added.

Administration comments suggest Trump is willing to increase taxes on U.S. consumers of EU-made products if the EU does not make unilateral concessions on tariffs, UBS' Paul Donovan said.

“Markets are unlikely to place too much weight on these threats given the president’s tendency to rapidly retreat on such issues, but the situation does (again) increase policy uncertainty,” Donovan added.

The economic calendar is quiet today with mainly the April new home sales on tap. Economists on average expect it to fall to 694K.

U.S. markets will be closed on Monday for Memorial Day.

data.title: Harvard barred from enrolling foreign students as standoff with Trump escalates
data.description:
The Trump administration has revoked Harvard University's ability to enroll international students, in the latest escalation of its standoff with the Ivy League school.

The Department of Homeland Security terminated Harvard's Student and Exchange Visitor Program (SEVP) certification. This means Harvard can no longer enroll foreign students and existing foreign students must transfer or lose their legal status.

"This administration is holding Harvard accountable for fostering violence, antisemitism, and coordinating with the Chinese Communist Party on its campus," said Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem. 

"It is a privilege, not a right, for universities to enroll foreign students and benefit from their higher tuition payments to help pad their multibillion-dollar endowments. Harvard had plenty of opportunity to do the right thing. It refused," she added.

The move follows Noem's demand last month for Harvard to provide information about the "criminality and misconduct of foreign students" on its campus, which the university refused. 

Harvard said the SEVP revocation is unlawful. "This retaliatory action threatens serious harm to the Harvard community and our country, and undermines Harvard’'s academic and research mission," a spokesperson said.

The Trump administration has already frozen over $2B in federal grants to Harvard this year, a move that the university is fighting in court.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Flutter announces pricing of senior secured notes
data.description:
Flutter (NYSE:FLUT) Friday announced the pricing of $1,000 million aggregate principal amount of 5.875% Senior Secured Notes due 2031, €550 million aggregate principal amount of 4.000% Senior Secured Notes due 2031.

It also announced the pricing of £450 million aggregate principal amount of 6.125% Senior Secured Notes due 2031.

An application will be made for the Notes to be admitted to trading on the International Stock Exchange.

The offering of the Notes is subject to customary closing conditions, and settlement is expected to occur on or around June 4, 2025.

data.title: International Paper to close 5 sites in UK, cut 300 jobs to streamline operations
European markets hold steady as traders await fresh cues; Bayer, Philips, SAP on the move
data.description:
London (UKX)  +0.40%

Retail sales in the United Kingdom advanced 5% year-on-year in April.

Germany (DAX:IND) +0.59%

France (CAC:IND)  +0.08%

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) rose 0.3% on Friday, rebounding after two consecutive sessions of losses. Despite the modest gains, the index was on track to end the week largely unchanged, as traders await fresh catalysts amid ongoing concerns over trade negotiations and the US fiscal outlook.

Meanwhile, Frankfurt's DAX edged up 0.5%, rising past 24,120 and putting it back on track for new all-time highs after Thursday’s pullback. 

On the corporate front, Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF), Philips (PHG), SAP (SAP), and Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY) were among the session’s top performers.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was down less than 1 basis point to 4.53%.

Germany's 10-year yield was down 2 basis points to 2.62%.

UK's 10-year yield was down 2 basis points to 4.74%. 

Coming up in the session: No major events lined up.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)
